Top picks

Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Propane or Natural Gas Island Grill – 864 sq. in. Cooking, 48,000 BTU Main + 12,000 BTU Side Burner, Porcelain Grates, Storage Cabinets, Semi-Portable - GM/Ash Stone

Built for serious backyard cooks who want island-style functionality without a permanent installation, the Tytus Fresno delivers 864 square inches of cooking space across five burners pushing 48,000 BTU, plus a dedicated 12,000 BTU side burner for sauces or sides. The semi-portable design and dual-fuel compatibility (propane or natural gas) make it unusually flexible for a grill at this size, and the porcelain grates and built-in storage cabinets give it the polish of a fixed outdoor kitchen. At $1,399.99 with a 4.2/5 rating across 16 reviews, it earns its price for buyers who want capacity and convenience in one unit.

The trade-off is that 16 reviews is a thin sample, so long-term durability data is limited compared to more established models. The GM/Ash Stone finish is distinctive but polarizing, and buyers who prefer a clean stainless look will need to look elsewhere. Semi-portable also means it won't satisfy those who want a fully fixed, drop-in installation.

Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island with Side Burner - 44,000 BTU Stainless Steel BBQ Grill Station with Storage Cabinets, Fold-out Prep Table, LPG/NG Convertible, Black

The Brand-Man 4-Burner Island is the best-reviewed complete grill station on this list, earning 4.7/5 from 113 reviews — a sample large enough to trust. At $799.99, it undercuts the Tytus Fresno by $600 while still delivering 44,000 BTU, a side burner, storage cabinets, and a fold-out prep table that adds meaningful workspace. LPG/NG convertibility means buyers aren't locked into one fuel source, and the black stainless finish suits modern outdoor kitchen builds.

Four burners at 44,000 BTU total means less output per burner than some competitors, which can matter when searing large cuts or running the grill at full capacity. The all-black aesthetic, while sharp, may show heat discoloration more visibly over time. It's a complete station rather than a built-in head, so buyers planning a fully integrated masonry kitchen will want a different format.

Monument Grills Larger 4+2 Burner Propane Gas Grills Stainless Steel Cabinet Style with Clearview® Lid, Knob Controls, Built-In Thermometer, Infrared Side Sear Burners & Side Burners, Blue

Monument's 4+2 burner configuration stands out for including infrared side sear burners alongside standard burners — a combination that gives serious grillers high-heat searing capability without buying a separate unit. The Clearview® lid, built-in thermometer, and cabinet-style base make it a well-equipped freestanding option, and at $519 it's the most affordable full-featured grill on this list. A perfect 5/5 from 71 reviews is a strong signal of consistent buyer satisfaction.

This is a propane-only model, which limits it for buyers specifically shopping for a natural gas connection. The blue color finish, while unique, is not a neutral choice and won't suit every patio aesthetic. Cabinet-style bases also tend to offer less versatile storage than purpose-built island configurations with proper cabinet doors and drawers.

Hygrill STD Series Heavy Duty 3-Burner Built In Grill, Stainless Steel Grill Head for Outdoor Kitchen, Natural Gas

Buyers building a custom outdoor kitchen who need a drop-in grill head rather than a freestanding station should look closely at the Hygrill STD Series. It's a dedicated natural gas built-in head — no conversion kit required — rated 5/5 from 41 reviews at $1,129.99. The heavy-duty stainless steel construction is built for a permanent installation where the surrounding structure handles storage and prep surface, making it a clean fit for masonry or cabinetry builds.

Three burners is a step down from the four- and five-burner competition at similar price points, so total cooking capacity is more limited. At $1,129.99 for a grill head alone — no island, no side burner, no prep table — the per-feature cost is high unless a custom build genuinely calls for this format. Buyers who aren't constructing a dedicated outdoor kitchen island will find freestanding options a better value.

Brand-Man Gas Grill Built-In Head, 30-Inch 4-Burner Propane Grill, Natural Gas Convertible, Heavy Duty 304 Stainless Steel 40,000BTUs BBQ Island Outdoor Kitchen

For built-in buyers who want more burners than the Hygrill STD Series without stepping up to the Tytus price point, the Brand-Man 30-inch built-in head offers four burners and 40,000 BTU in heavy-duty 304 stainless steel — a grade well-suited for permanent outdoor exposure. Natural gas convertibility is included, and the perfect 5/5 rating from 72 reviews at $889.99 gives it a stronger credibility base than many grill heads in this segment.

As a built-in head, it shares the same limitation as the Hygrill: no storage, no side burner, no prep surface unless the surrounding island provides them. Buyers who haven't planned that infrastructure will find the freestanding island-style grills more immediately usable. Propane is the default configuration, so natural gas buyers will need to complete the conversion before the first cook.

Stanbroil 28-Inch Premium Built-In Natural Gas Griddle for Outdoor Kitchen Grill, Durable Stainless Steel 2-Burner Flat Top Grill with Lid for BBQ Island, 34,000 BTUs

The Stanbroil 28-inch griddle is the only flat-top option on this list, making it the right pick for outdoor kitchens that prioritize smash burgers, breakfast cooking, stir-fry, or anything requiring a continuous cooking surface over open grates. It runs on natural gas natively, outputs 34,000 BTU across two burners, and includes a lid — a detail that matters for heat retention and weather protection. At $1,109.99 with a 4.5/5 from 20 reviews, it fills a specific niche that no other product here addresses.

A 2-burner flat-top griddle is a complement to a grill, not a replacement for one — buyers who want traditional grate cooking will need a separate unit. The 20-review sample is modest, and the price is high for a 28-inch two-burner surface. It's purpose-built for outdoor kitchen islands; anyone without a proper built-in cutout and a natural gas line already run will find it difficult to put to use. For more options in this category, see the Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen (2026) guide.

Buying guide

BTU output and cooking area are the two numbers that matter most, but neither means much without the other. A grill pumping 48,000 BTUs across 864 sq. in. of cooking surface — like the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner — can sustain high heat across a large cook without crowding. A smaller unit such as the Hygrill STD 3-Burner built-in delivers focused output for a dedicated island slot where raw square footage matters less than consistent, even heat. Match BTU-to-area ratio to how you actually cook: high-volume cooks need both, occasional weekend grilling can get by with less.

Fuel flexibility separates versatile grills from permanent installations. Several options here — including the Brand-Man 4-Burner Island and the Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head — are LPG/NG convertible, which means you can run them on propane now and switch to a natural gas line later without replacing the unit. If you're already piping in natural gas, a dedicated NG grill like the Hygrill STD or the Stanbroil 28-Inch Built-In Griddle locks in that convenience permanently. Buyers planning a full outdoor kitchen should also check whether the unit ships as a drop-in grill head or a freestanding island with cabinetry — those are fundamentally different purchases. For more on integrating grills into a permanent setup, see Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen (2026).

Cooking surface type is worth deliberate attention. Traditional grate-style grills with porcelain or stainless grates (the Tytus Fresno uses porcelain) suit classic grilling, while a flat-top griddle like the Stanbroil 28-Inch opens up smash burgers, seafood, and vegetables without flare-up risk. Infrared side sear burners — found on the Monument Grills 4+2 Burner — add steakhouse-level searing capability without upgrading the whole unit. Think about which cooking methods you'll actually use before defaulting to the highest BTU count.

Build quality and reviewer confidence should carry real weight. The Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head is constructed from heavy-duty 304 stainless steel and holds a 5/5 rating across 72 reviews — that combination of material spec and sustained buyer satisfaction is a meaningful signal. The Monument Grills 4+2 Burner and Hygrill STD both hold 5/5 ratings as well, with 71 and 41 reviews respectively. Contrast that with a lower price point and fewer reviews, and you have a clearer picture of where the proven value actually sits. Explore the full natural gas category at Natural Gas Outdoor Grills — Best Picks, Reviews & Guides if you want to compare beyond this shortlist.

Related guides

If you're building a full outdoor setup, the best gas grills for outdoor kitchens guide covers island-ready and built-in options in depth. Shoppers committed to natural gas should also check the best outdoor grills for natural gas roundup, which focuses specifically on NG-native and convertible models across a wider price range.

Frequently asked questions

What is a reasonable budget for a heavy-duty outdoor gas grill in 2026?

Capable heavy-duty outdoor gas grills start around $519 — the Monument Grills 4+2 Burner hits that price point with stainless steel construction, infrared side sear burners, and a clearview lid. Mid-tier island-style setups with storage cabinets and fold-out prep tables sit in the $800–$900 range, while premium built-in grill heads and full island systems with 48,000 BTU outputs climb to $1,100–$1,400. Expect to pay more as you move from freestanding units to true built-in heads designed for permanent outdoor kitchen installations.

What is the difference between a built-in grill head and a freestanding island grill?

A built-in grill head — like the Hygrill STD Series 3-Burner or the Brand-Man 30-Inch 4-Burner Built-In Head — is a drop-in unit designed to be set into a custom outdoor kitchen counter or BBQ island frame. It has no legs or cabinet base of its own. A freestanding island grill, such as the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner or the Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island, comes with its own cabinet structure, storage, and sometimes a prep table, so it can be positioned anywhere on a patio without additional construction.

Can these gas grills run on natural gas, or are they propane only?

Most models on this list support both. The Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island and the Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head are both explicitly listed as LPG/NG convertible. The Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Island and the Hygrill STD Series Built-In Grill are also offered in natural gas configurations. If you have a dedicated natural gas line, confirm the specific SKU or conversion kit is included before purchasing — some manufacturers ship a propane-configured unit with a separate NG orifice kit. For a deeper look at natural gas-specific models, see the Best Outdoor Grills Natural Gas (2026) guide.

How much cooking surface do I need for a large gathering?

A general rule: plan for roughly 72 square inches of primary cooking surface per person for a relaxed cookout pace. The Tytus Fresno 5-Burner tops this list at 864 sq. in. of total cooking area, which comfortably handles 10–12 people cooking simultaneously. The Brand-Man 4-Burner island and the Monument Grills 4+2 Burner both offer substantial main grates as well. For outdoor kitchens where a flat-top griddle is preferred over grates, the Stanbroil 28-Inch 2-Burner Built-In Griddle provides a wide flat cooking surface suited for batch cooking breakfast items, smash burgers, or stir-fry-style meals alongside a separate grill station.

Are stainless steel grates better than porcelain-coated grates?

Both have trade-offs. Bare stainless steel grates require seasoning but handle high heat without the risk of chipping, and they are easier to repair over time. Porcelain-coated grates — as found on the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner — offer a non-stick-like surface that is easier to clean when new, but porcelain can crack or chip if scrubbed aggressively or subjected to thermal shock, and once the coating is compromised the underlying metal can rust. For heavy, frequent use in a permanent outdoor kitchen, many buyers prefer heavy-gauge stainless grates. For occasional use where easy cleanup is the priority, porcelain holds up well.

Is a side burner worth the extra cost on an outdoor gas grill?

A side burner earns its place if the grill is your primary outdoor cooking station — it handles sauces, corn, baked beans, or anything that needs a pot or pan without forcing you back inside. The Tytus Fresno 5-Burner includes a 12,000 BTU side burner, the Brand-Man 4-Burner Island includes one as well, and the Monument Grills 4+2 Burner adds both infrared side sear burners and standard side burners. If space is limited and you are speccing a built-in head only, a side burner can always be added as a separate drop-in module in most outdoor kitchen island builds. The Tytus Fresno is built for serious backyard entertainers who want island-style cooking without committing to a permanent installation. Its 864 sq. in. of cooking surface paired with 48,000 BTU across the main burners gives it genuine high-heat capability, and the dual-fuel compatibility means it works whether you're running a natural gas line or using propane tanks. Porcelain-coated grates add durability and simplify cleanup, while the integrated storage cabinets keep tools and fuel out of sight. The semi-portable design is a genuine differentiator — it's one of the few island grills that can be repositioned without a full renovation.

At $1,399.99, it sits at the top of this list's price range, and with only 16 reviews (averaging 4.2/5), there's less real-world feedback to draw confidence from compared to competing models. The GM/Ash Stone finish is distinctive but polarizing — buyers who want a stainless-steel look will need to look elsewhere. The semi-portable format also means it lacks the structural permanence of a true built-in, so it may feel less solid than a fixed outdoor kitchen setup.

Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island with Side Burner - 44,000 BTU Stainless Steel BBQ Grill Station with Storage Cabinets, Fold-out Prep Table, LPG/NG Convertible, Black

The Brand-Man 4-Burner Grill Island is the strongest value pick in this lineup, with a 4.7/5 rating across 113 reviews — the most substantial feedback pool here. At $799.99, it delivers a full outdoor kitchen station: 44,000 BTU of cooking power, a dedicated side burner, fold-out prep table, and LPG/NG convertibility all in one package. The fold-out prep table is a practical feature that most competitors charge significantly more to include. Cooks who want a self-contained grill station without a major investment will find this hard to beat.

The black finish and stainless steel construction look sharp out of the box, but black powder-coat exteriors are more susceptible to fading and chipping under prolonged UV and heat exposure than brushed stainless. At 44,000 BTU across four burners, the per-burner output is adequate rather than exceptional — buyers who prioritize extreme searing heat should note that some competitors push higher BTU counts. It's also an island-format unit, so it requires dedicated outdoor space and isn't suited to compact patios or balconies.

Monument Grills Larger 4+2 Burner Propane Gas Grills Stainless Steel Cabinet Style with Clearview® Lid, Knob Controls, Built-In Thermometer, Infrared Side Sear Burners & Side Burners, Blue

Monument's 4+2 burner cabinet grill earns its perfect 5/5 score across 71 reviews, making it one of the most consistently praised options here. The combination of four main burners plus two infrared side sear burners is a genuine cooking advantage — infrared searing runs significantly hotter than standard burners and produces the kind of crust you'd normally need a dedicated sear station to achieve. The Clearview® lid lets you monitor food without lifting and venting heat, and the built-in thermometer adds basic heat management without requiring separate tools. At $519, it's the most affordable full-featured grill on this list.

This is a propane-only unit as listed, which matters for buyers planning around a natural gas line. The blue color option is uncommon in the outdoor grill category and won't suit every patio aesthetic. Cabinet-style propane grills of this price point also tend to use lighter-gauge steel in non-cooking components, so longevity under year-round exposure depends heavily on storage and cover habits. Buyers in harsh climates should factor in a quality grill cover.

Brand-Man Gas Grill Built-In Head, 30-Inch 4-Burner Propane Grill, Natural Gas Convertible, Heavy Duty 304 Stainless Steel 40,000BTUs BBQ Island Outdoor Kitchen

This Brand-Man built-in head is purpose-designed for outdoor kitchen installations, earning a 5/5 rating from 72 reviewers. The use of 304 stainless steel — a marine-grade alloy with strong corrosion resistance — is a meaningful spec distinction at the $889.99 price point, and it matters for longevity in humid or coastal environments. At 40,000 BTU across four burners in a 30-inch head, the output density is solid, and the natural gas convertibility means it integrates cleanly with a permanent gas line without requiring a separate conversion kit purchase.

As a built-in head only, it requires an existing outdoor kitchen island or a custom enclosure — buyers who don't already have that infrastructure will face significant additional costs before this grill is usable. The 30-inch width is on the compact end for a four-burner configuration, which translates to tighter cooking zones. It's also propane-primary with a conversion option, so buyers who want a native natural gas unit should confirm conversion kit availability and compatibility before purchasing.

Hygrill STD Series Heavy Duty 3-Burner Built In Grill, Stainless Steel Grill Head for Outdoor Kitchen, Natural Gas

The Hygrill STD Series targets buyers who want a native natural gas built-in without the propane-conversion compromise. Its 5/5 rating across 41 reviews reflects strong satisfaction for a purpose-built outdoor kitchen component. The "heavy duty" designation and stainless steel construction suggest a grill head built for long-term installation rather than seasonal use, and at $1,129.99, it sits in a tier where build quality expectations are appropriately high. For outdoor kitchens designed around a permanent natural gas supply, this is one of the most direct-fit options available.

Three burners is the most limited cooking capacity in this roundup, which constrains output for large gatherings. The price — $1,129.99 for a three-burner head — is high relative to competitors offering four burners at similar or lower cost, so the premium is justified only if natural gas native operation and build quality are the primary criteria. Like all built-in heads, it requires an existing island enclosure, adding cost and planning time for buyers starting from scratch. The smaller review count (41) compared to other five-star entries here means the rating has less statistical weight.

Stanbroil 28-Inch Premium Built-In Natural Gas Griddle for Outdoor Kitchen Grill, Durable Stainless Steel 2-Burner Flat Top Grill with Lid for BBQ Island, 34,000 BTUs

The Stanbroil 28-Inch Griddle is the only flat-top option in this lineup, making it the right choice for outdoor kitchens that need smash burgers, breakfast foods, stir-fry, and other cooking styles that a traditional grate simply can't handle. At 34,000 BTU across two burners, it delivers adequate heat for a 28-inch flat surface, and the stainless steel construction with a lid makes it a clean, permanent addition to a BBQ island. A 4.5/5 rating from 20 reviews shows early positive reception. Native natural gas operation means no conversion hassle for built-in setups.

A griddle cannot replace a grill — there's no open flame, no grate marks, and no wood-smoke transfer, so buyers who want traditional BBQ output need a separate grill alongside this unit. At $1,109.99 for a two-burner griddle head, the per-BTU cost is the highest in this group, and the 20-review sample is too small to draw firm conclusions about long-term durability. It's a specialized tool, not a primary grill, and should be budgeted as an addition to an outdoor kitchen rather than its centerpiece unless flat-top cooking is the explicit priority. For more options purpose-built for full outdoor kitchen setups, see the Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen (2026) guide.

Buying guide

BTU output sets the ceiling on heat and recovery speed, but the number alone doesn't tell the full story — cooking surface area matters just as much. The Tytus Fresno 5-Burner pairs 48,000 BTU across its main burners with 864 sq. in. of cooking space, which keeps BTU-per-square-inch density reasonable. The Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Grill Head delivers 40,000 BTUs on a 30-inch platform, a tighter ratio that translates to faster searing temperatures. As a rule, anything below roughly 80–90 BTU per square inch will struggle to reach and hold sear-level heat in cold or windy conditions.

Fuel flexibility separates versatile units from locked-in ones. Several grills here — the Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Island and the Brand-Man Built-In Head — are explicitly LPG/NG convertible, meaning you're not committed to one fuel source at install time. Grills like the Hygrill STD Series and the Stanbroil 28-Inch Natural Gas Griddle are natural gas native, so confirm your utility hookup before purchasing. If you're building a new outdoor kitchen and haven't run a gas line yet, convertible models give you more flexibility during construction.

Build material determines longevity under weather exposure. The Brand-Man Built-In Head specifies heavy-duty 304 stainless steel — the marine-grade alloy that resists rust better than the more common 430 stainless used in budget units. The Hygrill STD Series also markets itself as heavy-duty stainless construction. Porcelain-coated grates, as found on the Tytus Fresno, add non-stick convenience but can chip over time; solid stainless grates clean harder but last longer with less care.

Island integration versus freestanding setup is a decision that shapes your entire purchase path. Built-in heads like the Hygrill STD Series, the Brand-Man 30-Inch Head, and the Stanbroil griddle drop into existing masonry or prefab frames and don't include cabinetry — you supply the surround. Complete island stations like the Brand-Man 4-Burner Island and the Tytus Fresno ship with storage cabinets and prep surfaces already integrated, which saves fabrication cost but limits customization. For buyers linking this purchase to a larger outdoor kitchen build, a built-in head typically offers cleaner long-term results; for buyers who want a plug-and-cook setup, a complete island station is the faster path.

Frequently asked questions

What is a reasonable budget for a quality gas grill in 2026?

Expect to spend at least $500 for a well-built freestanding gas grill with multiple burners and a stainless steel cabinet. The Monument Grills 4+2 Burner at $519 sits near the entry point for cabinet-style grills with infrared side sear burners. Serious built-in grill heads and island setups run $800–$1,400, as seen with the Brand-Man 4-Burner Island ($799.99), the Hygrill STD Series Heavy Duty 3-Burner Built-In ($1,129.99), and the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Island ($1,399.99). Budget under $500 typically means compromises in grate quality, BTU output, or cabinet durability.

What is the difference between a built-in grill head and a freestanding grill?

A built-in grill head — like the Brand-Man Gas Grill Built-In Head 30-Inch or the Hygrill STD Series — is designed to drop into a pre-built outdoor kitchen island or counter cutout. It has no legs or cart and relies on the surrounding structure for support and storage. A freestanding grill ships as a complete unit with its own cabinet or cart, such as the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Island or the Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island. If you are building an outdoor kitchen from scratch and want a custom look, a grill head is the cleaner choice. If you want something ready to use immediately, a freestanding island grill is more practical.

Can I convert a propane gas grill to natural gas?

Many grills on this list support dual-fuel or LPG/NG conversion. The Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island is explicitly listed as LPG/NG convertible, and the Brand-Man Gas Grill Built-In Head is also natural gas convertible. The Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Island is listed as propane or natural gas. Conversion typically requires a separate conversion kit and adjustment of the orifices — confirm kit availability with the manufacturer before purchase. The Hygrill STD Series is listed specifically as a natural gas model. Always verify that the conversion maintains the stated BTU ratings and does not void the warranty.

How much cooking surface do I need for a family of four to six people?

A primary cooking area of 400–500 square inches handles four to six people comfortably for most grilling scenarios. The Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Island leads this list at 864 sq. in. of total cooking surface, which is more than adequate for large gatherings. For a standard family cookout, the Monument Grills 4+2 Burner and the Brand-Man built-in options offer enough primary grate space without excessive footprint. If you regularly cook for crowds of eight or more, prioritize grills with 600+ sq. in. or a dedicated warming rack in addition to the primary grate.

Which gas grill has the best user ratings among the options listed?

Three grills on this list carry a 5/5 rating: the Monument Grills Larger 4+2 Burner (71 reviews), the Brand-Man Gas Grill Built-In Head 30-Inch (72 reviews), and the Hygrill STD Series Heavy Duty 3-Burner Built-In (41 reviews). Among those, the Monument and Brand-Man built-in head have the highest review counts, giving those ratings stronger statistical weight. The Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island scores 4.7/5 across 113 reviews — the largest review pool of any product listed — making it one of the most broadly validated options for buyers who want consensus-backed confidence.

Is a flat top griddle a good alternative to a traditional gas grill for outdoor kitchens?

A flat top griddle excels at breakfast foods, smash burgers, stir-fry, and anything that benefits from a large, even cooking surface and the ability to retain drippings. The Stanbroil 28-Inch Premium Built-In Natural Gas Griddle delivers 34,000 BTUs across two burners and is designed to drop into an outdoor kitchen island like a traditional built-in grill head. What it cannot do is impart direct flame char or grill marks. Many serious outdoor kitchen builds include both a grill and a griddle side by side. For buyers who cook varied menus, the Stanbroil makes a strong complement to any of the built-in grill heads above rather than a straight replacement. title: Natural Gas For Grills
metaDescription: Natural Gas For Grills. Editor's picks, in-depth review, and comparisons for "natural gas for grills".
outline:
Disclosure: This page contains affiliate links. We may earn a commission if you purchase through them — at no extra cost to you. Recommendations are based on independent testing and editorial judgment.

Natural gas for grills means running your outdoor grill directly from a home gas line — a permanent, cost-effective setup that matters most to homeowners who grill frequently and want to ditch the hassle of swapping propane tanks. For a full breakdown of compatible gr

What it is and how it works

Natural gas for grills is exactly what the name says: methane-based utility gas — the same fuel piped into your home for the furnace or kitchen range — routed directly to an outdoor grill through a dedicated gas line. Instead of swapping out a 20-lb propane tank every few cookouts, a natural gas grill connects permanently to your home's supply, meaning the fuel never runs out mid-cook. A backyard built-in grill plumbed into the house line is the clearest example: you open the valve, ignite the burners, and there is no cylinder to monitor or haul to the exchange rack.

Natural gas and propane are both hydrocarbon fuels, but they are not interchangeable without modification. Natural gas has a lower energy density — roughly 1,020 BTU per cubic foot versus propane's 2,516 BTU per cubic foot — so natural gas orifices (the small brass fittings that meter gas into each burner) are drilled larger to compensate and deliver the same heat output. Grills sold as natural gas models ship with these wider orifices already installed. Attempting to run natural gas through a propane orifice starves the burner; doing the reverse creates a dangerous over-rich flame.

The delivery path from the street to the cooking grate follows a straightforward sequence. The utility company supplies gas at relatively high pressure through the service line. A regulator at or near the meter steps that pressure down to a safe working level — typically around 7 inches of water column (roughly 0.25 psi) for residential appliances. From there, a licensed plumber runs a black iron or CSST (corrugated stainless steel tubing) line to a shutoff valve at the grill location. The grill's own internal regulator makes any final pressure adjustment, and the manifold distributes gas to each individual burner valve. When a burner knob is turned, gas flows through that valve, passes the igniter, and burns in a controlled flame at the cooking surface. Because the supply is continuous and pressure-regulated at multiple points, heat output stays consistent from the first minute of a cook to the last — something a depleting propane tank cannot guarantee.

For anyone planning an outdoor kitchen setup, understanding this infrastructure is the first practical step. The line sizing, shutoff placement, and regulator spec all need to match the total BTU load of every appliance on that run. More detail on configuring those setups is covered in the guide to best gas grills for outdoor kitchens, and a broader look at how different natural gas models compare starts with the natural gas outdoor grills hub.

Examples

A homeowner with a built-in outdoor kitchen connects a 36-inch natural gas grill directly to the house gas line via a dedicated 1/2-inch black iron pipe run from the meter. The grill never needs a propane tank swap mid-cook, and the supply pressure stays consistent even during a long, slow brisket session. This setup is the standard approach for any gas grill built into an outdoor kitchen.

A neighbor on a large suburban lot wants a freestanding cart grill on a patio 40 feet from the house. A licensed plumber installs a 3/4-inch corrugated stainless steel tubing (CSST) line buried underground to a quick-disconnect valve at the patio edge. The grill plugs into that valve with a flexible connector rated for outdoor natural gas use, meeting code requirements in most jurisdictions.

A restaurant operator running a high-volume outdoor cookout station needs continuous BTU output that a standard 20-lb propane cylinder simply cannot sustain. Tapping the commercial gas main and running a dedicated line to a heavy-duty outdoor natural gas grill eliminates both the cost of bulk propane delivery and the pressure drop that occurs when a cylinder runs low. The result is steady flame output across all burners regardless of how long the grill runs.

Related concepts

If you're planning a built-in setup, the guides on the best gas grills for outdoor kitchens and the best grills built specifically for natural gas cover the hardware decisions that follow once your gas line is in place. For a broader look at how natural gas grills compare across price points and styles, the natural gas outdoor grills hub is the logical next stop.

Frequently asked questions

Can I convert a propane grill to natural gas?

Many grills can be converted, but only if the manufacturer offers an approved conversion kit for that specific model. Conversion involves swapping the orifices and regulator to handle natural gas's lower pressure and different BTU characteristics. Attempting a DIY conversion without the correct kit is a safety risk and will void most warranties. Check with the manufacturer before purchasing any third-party kit.

Do natural gas grills need a regulator?

Yes. Natural gas arrives at your home at varying line pressures depending on your utility provider. A dedicated regulator — typically built into the grill or the quick-connect fitting — steps that down to the consistent low pressure the burners require. Never connect a natural gas grill directly to a house line without a properly rated regulator in place.

How far does the grill need to be from my house?

Most local codes require a minimum of 10 feet of clearance from any structure, combustible siding, or overhead covering. Some jurisdictions allow built-in outdoor kitchen installations closer to walls when proper non-combustible materials are used. Always check local fire codes and the grill manufacturer's clearance requirements before finalizing placement.

What size natural gas line do I need for a grill?

The required pipe diameter depends on the total BTU load of the grill and the length of the run from the gas meter. A high-output grill rated at 60,000 BTU or more on a long run will need at least a ¾-inch line to avoid pressure drop. A licensed plumber or gas fitter should size and install the line — undersized pipe causes weak flames, uneven heating, and potential flameout.

Is natural gas cheaper to run than propane for grilling?

In most parts of the United States, natural gas costs significantly less per BTU than propane. The trade-off is the upfront cost of running a dedicated gas line and purchasing a natural gas–specific or converted grill. Frequent grillers who cook year-round typically recoup that installation cost within a few seasons compared to buying and refilling propane tanks regularly.

Can a natural gas grill be used with an outdoor kitchen setup?

Natural gas is actually the preferred fuel for built-in outdoor kitchens because it eliminates the need to store or swap propane tanks beneath or around cabinetry. Drop-in and built-in grill heads designed for outdoor kitchens are widely available in natural gas configurations. For more on pairing grills with full outdoor kitchen builds, see Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen (2026).

Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Propane or Natural Gas Island Grill – 864 sq. in. Cooking, 48,000 BTU Main + 12,000 BTU Side Burner, Porcelain Grates, Storage Cabinets, Semi-Portable - GM/Ash Stone

At 864 sq. in. of cooking area, it handles large gatherings without crowding. Porcelain grates and storage cabinets round out a package that punches well above its price point.

The trade-off is a modest review count — 16 ratings at 4.2/5 — meaning long-term durability data is thinner than on more established models. The GM/Ash Stone finish also narrows aesthetic compatibility for outdoor kitchens with modern stainless or dark cabinetry. At $1,399.99 it sits at the higher end of this lineup, so buyers who don't need the semi-portable feature or full island cabinet system may find better value elsewhere.

Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island with Side Burner - 44,000 BTU Stainless Steel BBQ Grill Station with Storage Cabinets, Fold-out Prep Table, LPG/NG Convertible, Black

The Brand-Man 4-Burner island station earns its 4.7/5 score across 113 reviews — one of the stronger confidence signals in this group. LPG/NG convertibility means it adapts to whatever fuel source is already run to the patio. The black stainless finish suits contemporary outdoor kitchen aesthetics well.

The 44,000 BTU output, while solid for everyday grilling, trails the Tytus Fresno and the Brand-Man Built-In Head for high-heat searing applications. Buyers who prioritize maximum BTU or need a drop-in head for a custom island build will find this freestanding station too rigid — the fold-out table and fixed cabinet layout aren't designed for integration into poured concrete or tile surrounds.

Brand-Man Gas Grill Built-In Head, 30-Inch 4-Burner Propane Grill, Natural Gas Convertible, Heavy Duty 304 Stainless Steel 40,000BTUs BBQ Island Outdoor Kitchen

A perfect 5/5 across 72 reviews is a meaningful signal, and the Brand-Man Built-In Head backs it up with a spec sheet suited to serious island builds. The 304 stainless steel construction is the standout detail — that alloy grade resists corrosion better than lower-grade steel and is the benchmark material for built-in outdoor cooking equipment. At 40,000 BTU across four burners in a 30-inch drop-in format, it fits standard island cutout dimensions and converts cleanly between propane and natural gas. At $889.99 it offers legitimate value for a purpose-built island head.

As a grill head only, it requires an existing or planned island surround — there are no storage cabinets, prep surface, or side burner included. Buyers who want a turnkey station should look at the Brand-Man island station above. Buying guide and further reading

BTU output sets the ceiling on how fast a grill recovers heat between loads, but raw numbers only mean something relative to cooking area. The Tytus Fresno delivers 48,000 BTU main-burner output across 864 sq. in. of cooking space — a reasonable ratio for a large island build. The Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island puts out 44,000 BTU over a more compact footprint, which translates to faster surface temperatures for everyday cooks. If the station will see high-volume use, prioritize BTU-per-square-inch density over total BTU alone.

Burner count and configuration matter more than many buyers expect. A dedicated side burner — present on both the Tytus Fresno (12,000 BTU) and the Brand-Man island station — keeps sauces and sides off the main grate without monopolizing primary cooking real estate. For pure flat-top work, the Stanbroil 28-Inch Built-In Natural Gas Griddle offers a 2-burner, 34,000 BTU setup specifically optimized for flat-surface cooking rather than grate grilling, which is a fundamentally different use case. Match the burner layout to how the station will actually be used, not just to the highest number on the spec sheet.

Fuel flexibility and construction grade are the two specs most likely to affect long-term ownership. Several options here — including the Brand-Man built-in head and the Monument Grills 4+2 Burner — are LPG/NG convertible, which matters if the installation site doesn't have a permanent gas line yet or if the setup might move. On materials, the Brand-Man Built-In Head and Hygrill STD Series both specify 304 stainless steel, a meaningful distinction over lesser grades that corrode faster under outdoor exposure and high heat cycling. Porcelain-coated grates, as found on the Tytus Fresno, are easier to clean but require more careful handling than raw cast iron or stainless alternatives.

Storage and prep infrastructure often gets underweighted at purchase. Enclosed storage cabinets — standard on the Tytus Fresno, Brand-Man island, and others — keep propane tanks, tools, and accessories sheltered from weather. The Brand-Man 4-Burner island adds a fold-out prep table, which is genuinely useful in kitchens where counter space is tight. Budget for these features deliberately rather than treating them as extras, since retrofitting cabinetry to an existing built-in is expensive.

For deeper guidance on drop-in and built-in configurations specifically, Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen (2026) covers what to look for when designing a permanent island setup. The broader roundup at Best Barbeque Grills Natural Gas (2026) is worth consulting if the choice between natural gas and propane is still open, and Best Outdoor Grills Natural Gas (2026) goes further into freestanding versus built-in trade-offs for natural gas buyers.

Frequently asked questions

What is the minimum budget for a decent outdoor kitchen barbecue grill?

The Monument Grills Larger 4+2 Burner cabinet-style grill comes in at $519 and earns a 5/5 rating across 71 reviews, making it the lowest entry point among quality options here. For a full island station with storage cabinets and a side burner, the Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island at $799.99 is the next step up. Expect to spend at least $1,100–$1,400 for a built-in grill head rated for permanent outdoor kitchen installation in stainless steel.

Can these grills run on both propane and natural gas?

Most models in this category are dual-fuel convertible. The Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island and the Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head are both explicitly listed as LPG/NG convertible. The Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Island also supports propane or natural gas. The Hygrill STD Series Built-In Grill Head is listed as natural gas only, so confirm the fuel type before purchasing if you need propane flexibility.

What is the difference between an island grill and a built-in grill head?

An island grill — like the Tytus Fresno or the Brand-Man 4-Burner island station — arrives as a complete unit with storage cabinets, a countertop frame, and sometimes a fold-out prep table. A built-in grill head, such as the Brand-Man 30-Inch or the Hygrill STD Series, is just the cooking unit itself, designed to drop into a custom masonry or modular outdoor kitchen frame you already own or are building. Built-in heads give more design flexibility but require additional construction cost.

How much BTU output do I actually need for outdoor grilling?

For a standard family cook, 40,000–48,000 BTU across four to five main burners is more than sufficient. The Tytus Fresno delivers 48,000 BTU on the main burners plus a 12,000 BTU side burner. The Brand-Man 4-Burner island puts out 44,000 BTU. The Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head reaches 40,000 BTU. Higher BTU ratings matter most for fast preheat and high-heat searing; they become less important for low-and-slow cooking where you want precise low-end control.

Which grill is best suited for a permanent outdoor kitchen installation?

The Hygrill STD Series Heavy Duty 3-Burner Built-In Grill Head and the Brand-Man 30-Inch 4-Burner Built-In Head are purpose-built for permanent drop-in installation. Both use heavy-duty 304 stainless steel construction and are designed to integrate flush with countertop surfaces. The Stanbroil 28-Inch Natural Gas Griddle is a strong companion or alternative for flat-top cooking in the same built-in format, rated at 34,000 BTU with a lid. For buyers who want a natural gas line run directly to the unit, all three of these built-in options support that configuration.

Is a flat-top griddle worth including in an outdoor kitchen alongside a grill?

A dedicated flat-top adds genuine cooking versatility — smash burgers, breakfast items, stir-fry, and delicate fish all cook better on a griddle than over open flame. The Stanbroil 28-Inch Premium Built-In Natural Gas Griddle at $1,109.99 is the only purpose-built griddle option in this lineup. At 34,000 BTU across two burners and 28 inches wide, it fits standard outdoor kitchen module widths and pairs cleanly next to a built-in grill head without requiring a separate fuel connection if you are already running natural gas.

outline:
Disclosure: This page contains affiliate links. We may earn a commission if you purchase through them — at no extra cost to you. Recommendations are based on independent testing and editorial judgment.

Natural gas outdoor grills cover everything from freestanding cabinet-style units to built-in grill heads and complete island stations designed for permanent outdoor kitchens. Shoppers here range from homeowners running a gas line to an existing patio to serious backyard cooks building a full outdoor kitchen with storage, side burners, and integrated griddles. The core decision dimensions are configuration (built-in head vs. freestanding vs. full island), BTU output and burner count, cooking surface area, and whether the unit ships ready for natural gas or requires a propane-to-NG conversion kit. Material quality — particularly 304 stainless steel construction and porcelain or stainless grates — separates entry-level picks from heavy-duty options built to handle daily use and weather exposure year-round. For buyers focused on specific use cases, see the dedicated guides on best gas grills for outdoor kitchens, best barbeque grills in natural gas, and the broader best outdoor grills natural gas roundup.

Subcategories

Built-In Grills for Outdoor Kitchens

This cluster covers grill heads and built-in units designed to drop into a stone, concrete, or steel island frame — the kind of permanent setup where aesthetics and BTU output both matter. Products like the Hygrill STD Series Heavy Duty 3-Burner Built In Grill and the Brand-Man Gas Grill Built-In Head fall squarely here, offering stainless steel construction and direct natural gas hookups that eliminate the need for propane tanks. It's the right category if you're building or upgrading a fixed outdoor kitchen rather than buying a freestanding cart grill. Explore the best gas grills for outdoor kitchens.

Freestanding and Island Natural Gas Grills

For buyers who want a complete, self-contained grilling station — cabinets, side burners, prep tables, and all — this subcategory focuses on full island and cart-style natural gas grills. Units like the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Island Grill and the Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island with Side Burner combine high BTU output with built-in storage, making them practical for frequent entertainers who don't want to integrate into masonry. Most models in this group are LPG/NG convertible, so they work whether your yard is plumbed for natural gas or not. See the top-rated outdoor natural gas grills.

BBQ and Barbecue Natural Gas Grills

This group targets traditional barbecue-style cooking on natural gas — think multi-burner setups with porcelain or stainless grates, high-capacity cooking surfaces, and classic cabinet styling. It overlaps with freestanding grills but skews toward buyers who prioritize low-and-slow BBQ performance alongside searing ability, rather than integrated kitchen functionality. Browse the best natural gas barbeque grills or check the closely related barbecue grills natural gas guide for additional picks and comparisons.

Featured products

The Hygrill STD Series Heavy Duty 3-Burner Built In Grill, Stainless Steel Grill Head for Outdoor Kitchen, Natural Gas earns a perfect 5/5 from 41 reviewers and is built specifically for drop-in outdoor kitchen installations, with a heavy-duty stainless steel head priced at $1,129.99. It's a strong pick for homeowners who want a clean, integrated look without sacrificing build quality.

The Brand-Man Gas Grill Built-In Head, 30-Inch 4-Burner Propane Grill, Natural Gas Convertible, Heavy Duty 304 Stainless Steel 40,000BTUs BBQ Island Outdoor Kitchen delivers 40,000 BTUs across four burners with 304-grade stainless steel construction, and at $889.99 it holds a perfect 5/5 rating across 72 reviews — the highest review count among the built-in heads here. Its natural gas convertibility adds long-term flexibility.

For buyers who want a complete outdoor cooking station rather than a grill head alone, the Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island with Side Burner - 44,000 BTU Stainless Steel BBQ Grill Station with Storage Cabinets, Fold-out Prep Table, LPG/NG Convertible, Black bundles 44,000 BTUs, a side burner, fold-out prep table, and enclosed storage cabinets into a single unit at $799.99, backed by 113 reviews averaging 4.7/5.

The Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Propane or Natural Gas Island Grill is the highest-output option in this group, combining a 48,000 BTU main burner array with a 12,000 BTU side burner and 864 sq. in. of cooking space across porcelain grates — all in a semi-portable island format with storage cabinets at $1,399.99. It suits large households or anyone who regularly cooks for a crowd. For more complete outdoor kitchen setups, see the Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen guide.

The Stanbroil 28-Inch Premium Built-In Natural Gas Griddle for Outdoor Kitchen Grill, Durable Stainless Steel 2-Burner Flat Top Grill with Lid for BBQ Island, 34,000 BTUs fills a specific niche: a flat-top griddle surface rather than grate cooking, making it ideal for breakfast items, smash burgers, or seafood. Its 34,000 BTUs across two burners and lidded design are uncommon in built-in natural gas griddles at this $1,109.99 price point.

The Monument Grills Larger 4+2 Burner Propane Gas Grills Stainless Steel Cabinet Style with Clearview® Lid, Knob Controls, Built-In Thermometer, Infrared Side Sear Burners & Side Burners, Blue is the most affordable option at $519 and packs infrared side sear burners alongside two additional side burners — a feature set that typically appears on grills costing considerably more. Its Clearview® lid and 5/5 rating from 71 buyers make it a compelling freestanding alternative. Browse additional options in the Best Outdoor Grills Natural Gas and Best Barbeque Grills Natural Gas roundups.

Common questions in this category

Is a natural gas grill actually worth it over propane?

For anyone who cooks outdoors regularly, a direct natural gas connection eliminates the hassle of monitoring and swapping propane tanks — fuel is always on and the cost per BTU is typically lower than propane over time. The trade-off is that the grill must sit within reach of a gas line, so placement is fixed rather than flexible. If your outdoor space is permanent and you grill more than a few times a month, the convenience and long-term savings usually justify the conversion or upfront purchase.

See the top-rated natural gas outdoor grills →

What should you look for when buying a built-in grill for an outdoor kitchen?

Built-in grills need to fit a specific cabinet cutout, hold up to weather without a cover-friendly design, and be made from marine-grade or 304 stainless steel so they don't rust flush against surrounding stonework or cabinetry. BTU output, cooking area, and whether the unit ships ready for natural gas (or requires a conversion kit) are the other critical specs to confirm before ordering. A grill head rated for outdoor kitchen installation is not interchangeable with a freestanding unit — the ventilation, trim, and mounting requirements differ substantially.

Explore the best gas grills for outdoor kitchens →

How many BTUs do you actually need for outdoor grilling?

BTU ratings are useful for comparison but misleading in isolation — a well-designed 40,000 BTU grill with good heat retention will outperform a poorly built 60,000 BTU model. A practical rule of thumb is 80–100 BTUs per square inch of primary cooking area, which means a 400 sq. in. grill needs roughly 32,000–40,000 BTUs to sear and recover temperature quickly. Side burners, infrared sear zones, and griddle surfaces each carry separate BTU specs and should be evaluated independently from the main burner output.

Browse high-BTU natural gas barbecue grills →

Frequently asked questions

What is the difference between a built-in grill head and a freestanding natural gas grill?

A built-in grill head drops into a cutout in an outdoor kitchen island or counter, leaving only the cooking surface exposed. Models like the Hygrill STD Series and the Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head are designed specifically for this installation. Freestanding grills sit on their own cart or legs and can be repositioned. If you are planning a permanent outdoor kitchen, a built-in head gives a cleaner look and is generally easier to plumb to a natural gas line. If flexibility matters more than aesthetics, a freestanding or semi-portable option is the better fit.

Do I need a dedicated natural gas line, or can I convert a propane grill?

Most heavy-duty grills in this category are either factory-configured for natural gas or sold as convertible units. The Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head, for example, is listed as natural gas convertible, and the Brand-Man 4-Burner Grill Island is rated for both LPG and NG. Conversion typically requires a separate orifice kit and, in most municipalities, a licensed plumber or gas fitter to tap into the supply line. Converting without proper hardware voids warranties and creates a safety hazard, so verify the kit is included or available before purchasing a propane-primary model.

How do I choose between a grill head only and a full grill island station?

A grill head is the right buy when you already have an island structure or are building a custom outdoor kitchen with specific cabinet and countertop materials. A full island station, like the Brand-Man 4-Burner Grill Island with storage cabinets and fold-out prep table or the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Island Grill, bundles storage, prep space, and sometimes a side burner into one purchase. Island stations cost less than sourcing each component separately but limit your design choices. The trade-off is convenience and speed of setup versus full customization.

What BTU output should I look for in a heavy-duty outdoor gas grill?

For everyday grilling, roughly 10,000 BTU per burner is a reasonable baseline. The grills reviewed across this silo range from 34,000 BTU across two burners on the Stanbroil Built-In Griddle to 48,000 BTU on the Tytus Fresno main burners plus a 12,000 BTU side burner. Higher BTU matters most for searing and recovering temperature quickly after you open the lid. However, total BTU is only meaningful in context — a well-insulated cooking chamber with tightly fitting grates will outperform a higher-BTU grill with poor heat retention. Factor in cooking area alongside BTU when comparing models.

Which subcategory pages should I check based on how I plan to use the grill?

The right starting point depends on your setup. If you are building or upgrading a full outdoor kitchen, Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen covers built-in and island-compatible options in depth. For a broader look at standalone and semi-portable natural gas units, Best Outdoor Grills Natural Gas is the most comprehensive overview. Buyers focused specifically on backyard barbecue performance should see Best Barbeque Grills Natural Gas or Best Barbecue Grills Natural Gas. If compatibility with an existing natural gas hookup is the primary concern, Best Grills For Natural Gas filters by that criterion directly.

Are stainless steel grills actually more durable outdoors, and which grade matters?

Stainless steel resists rust and surface corrosion far better than painted steel in outdoor environments, but the grade determines how well it holds up over years of use. Grade 304 stainless — used in the Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head — contains higher nickel and chromium content than the more common 430 grade, making it more resistant to pitting in humid or coastal environments. For a grill that will stay permanently installed and exposed to weather year-round, 304 stainless is worth the premium. For covered or seasonal use, 430 stainless performs adequately at a lower cost.

title: Outdoor Grills
metaDescription: Outdoor Grills. Editor's picks, in-depth review, and comparisons for "outdoor grills".

Disclosure: This page contains affiliate links. We may earn a commission if you purchase through them — at no extra cost to you. Recommendations are based on independent testing and editorial judgment.

Natural gas outdoor grills cover everything from freestanding cabinet-style units to built-in grill heads and complete island stations designed for permanent outdoor kitchens. Shoppers here range from homeowners running a gas line to an existing patio to serious backyard cooks building a full outdoor kitchen with storage, side burners, and integrated griddles. The core decision dimensions are configuration (built-in head vs. freestanding vs. full island), BTU output and burner count, cooking surface area, and whether the unit ships ready for natural gas or requires a propane-to-NG conversion kit. Material quality — particularly 304 stainless steel construction and porcelain or stainless grates — separates entry-level picks from heavy-duty options built to handle daily use and weather exposure year-round. For buyers focused on specific use cases, see the dedicated guides on best gas grills for outdoor kitchens, best barbeque grills in natural gas, and the broader best outdoor grills natural gas roundup.

Subcategories

Built-In Grills for Outdoor Kitchens

This cluster covers grill heads and built-in units designed to drop into a stone, concrete, or steel island frame — the kind of permanent setup where aesthetics and BTU output both matter. Products like the Hygrill STD Series Heavy Duty 3-Burner Built In Grill and the Brand-Man Gas Grill Built-In Head fall squarely here, offering stainless steel construction and direct natural gas hookups that eliminate the need for propane tanks. It's the right category if you're building or upgrading a fixed outdoor kitchen rather than buying a freestanding cart grill. Explore the best gas grills for outdoor kitchens.

Freestanding and Island Natural Gas Grills

For buyers who want a complete, self-contained grilling station — cabinets, side burners, prep tables, and all — this subcategory focuses on full island and cart-style natural gas grills. Units like the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Island Grill and the Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island with Side Burner combine high BTU output with built-in storage, making them practical for frequent entertainers who don't want to integrate into masonry. Most models in this group are LPG/NG convertible, so they work whether your yard is plumbed for natural gas or not. See the top-rated outdoor natural gas grills.

BBQ and Barbecue Natural Gas Grills

This group targets traditional barbecue-style cooking on natural gas — think multi-burner setups with porcelain or stainless grates, high-capacity cooking surfaces, and classic cabinet styling. It overlaps with freestanding grills but skews toward buyers who prioritize low-and-slow BBQ performance alongside searing ability, rather than integrated kitchen functionality. Browse the best natural gas barbeque grills or check the closely related barbecue grills natural gas guide for additional picks and comparisons.

Featured products

The Hygrill STD Series Heavy Duty 3-Burner Built In Grill, Stainless Steel Grill Head for Outdoor Kitchen, Natural Gas earns a perfect 5/5 from 41 reviewers and is built specifically for drop-in outdoor kitchen installations, with a heavy-duty stainless steel head priced at $1,129.99. It's a strong pick for homeowners who want a clean, integrated look without sacrificing build quality.

The Brand-Man Gas Grill Built-In Head, 30-Inch 4-Burner Propane Grill, Natural Gas Convertible, Heavy Duty 304 Stainless Steel 40,000BTUs BBQ Island Outdoor Kitchen delivers 40,000 BTUs across four burners with 304-grade stainless steel construction, and at $889.99 it holds a perfect 5/5 rating across 72 reviews — the highest review count among the built-in heads here. Its natural gas convertibility adds long-term flexibility.

For buyers who want a complete outdoor cooking station rather than a grill head alone, the Brand-Man 4-Burner Gas Outdoor Kitchen Grill Island with Side Burner - 44,000 BTU Stainless Steel BBQ Grill Station with Storage Cabinets, Fold-out Prep Table, LPG/NG Convertible, Black bundles 44,000 BTUs, a side burner, fold-out prep table, and enclosed storage cabinets into a single unit at $799.99, backed by 113 reviews averaging 4.7/5.

The Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Propane or Natural Gas Island Grill is the highest-output option in this group, combining a 48,000 BTU main burner array with a 12,000 BTU side burner and 864 sq. in. of cooking space across porcelain grates — all in a semi-portable island format with storage cabinets at $1,399.99. It suits large households or anyone who regularly cooks for a crowd. For more complete outdoor kitchen setups, see the Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen guide.

The Stanbroil 28-Inch Premium Built-In Natural Gas Griddle for Outdoor Kitchen Grill, Durable Stainless Steel 2-Burner Flat Top Grill with Lid for BBQ Island, 34,000 BTUs fills a specific niche: a flat-top griddle surface rather than grate cooking, making it ideal for breakfast items, smash burgers, or seafood. Its 34,000 BTUs across two burners and lidded design are uncommon in built-in natural gas griddles at this $1,109.99 price point.

The Monument Grills Larger 4+2 Burner Propane Gas Grills Stainless Steel Cabinet Style with Clearview® Lid, Knob Controls, Built-In Thermometer, Infrared Side Sear Burners & Side Burners, Blue is the most affordable option at $519 and packs infrared side sear burners alongside two additional side burners — a feature set that typically appears on grills costing considerably more. Its Clearview® lid and 5/5 rating from 71 buyers make it a compelling freestanding alternative. Browse additional options in the Best Outdoor Grills Natural Gas and Best Barbeque Grills Natural Gas roundups.

Common questions in this category

Is a natural gas grill actually worth it over propane?

For anyone who cooks outdoors regularly, a direct natural gas connection eliminates the hassle of monitoring and swapping propane tanks — fuel is always on and the cost per BTU is typically lower than propane over time. The trade-off is that the grill must sit within reach of a gas line, so placement is fixed rather than flexible. If your outdoor space is permanent and you grill more than a few times a month, the convenience and long-term savings usually justify the conversion or upfront purchase.

See the top-rated natural gas outdoor grills →

What should you look for when buying a built-in grill for an outdoor kitchen?

Built-in grills need to fit a specific cabinet cutout, hold up to weather without a cover-friendly design, and be made from marine-grade or 304 stainless steel so they don't rust flush against surrounding stonework or cabinetry. BTU output, cooking area, and whether the unit ships ready for natural gas (or requires a conversion kit) are the other critical specs to confirm before ordering. A grill head rated for outdoor kitchen installation is not interchangeable with a freestanding unit — the ventilation, trim, and mounting requirements differ substantially.

Explore the best gas grills for outdoor kitchens →

How many BTUs do you actually need for outdoor grilling?

BTU ratings are useful for comparison but misleading in isolation — a well-designed 40,000 BTU grill with good heat retention will outperform a poorly built 60,000 BTU model. A practical rule of thumb is 80–100 BTUs per square inch of primary cooking area, which means a 400 sq. in. grill needs roughly 32,000–40,000 BTUs to sear and recover temperature quickly. Side burners, infrared sear zones, and griddle surfaces each carry separate BTU specs and should be evaluated independently from the main burner output.

Browse high-BTU natural gas barbecue grills →

Frequently asked questions

What is the difference between a built-in grill head and a freestanding natural gas grill?

A built-in grill head drops into a cutout in an outdoor kitchen island or counter, leaving only the cooking surface exposed. Models like the Hygrill STD Series and the Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head are designed specifically for this installation. Freestanding grills sit on their own cart or legs and can be repositioned. If you are planning a permanent outdoor kitchen, a built-in head gives a cleaner look and is generally easier to plumb to a natural gas line. If flexibility matters more than aesthetics, a freestanding or semi-portable option is the better fit.

Do I need a dedicated natural gas line, or can I convert a propane grill?

Most heavy-duty grills in this category are either factory-configured for natural gas or sold as convertible units. The Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head, for example, is listed as natural gas convertible, and the Brand-Man 4-Burner Grill Island is rated for both LPG and NG. Conversion typically requires a separate orifice kit and, in most municipalities, a licensed plumber or gas fitter to tap into the supply line. Converting without proper hardware voids warranties and creates a safety hazard, so verify the kit is included or available before purchasing a propane-primary model.

How do I choose between a grill head only and a full grill island station?

A grill head is the right buy when you already have an island structure or are building a custom outdoor kitchen with specific cabinet and countertop materials. A full island station, like the Brand-Man 4-Burner Grill Island with storage cabinets and fold-out prep table or the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Island Grill, bundles storage, prep space, and sometimes a side burner into one purchase. Island stations cost less than sourcing each component separately but limit your design choices. The trade-off is convenience and speed of setup versus full customization.

What BTU output should I look for in a heavy-duty outdoor gas grill?

For everyday grilling, roughly 10,000 BTU per burner is a reasonable baseline. The grills reviewed across this silo range from 34,000 BTU across two burners on the Stanbroil Built-In Griddle to 48,000 BTU on the Tytus Fresno main burners plus a 12,000 BTU side burner. Higher BTU matters most for searing and recovering temperature quickly after you open the lid. However, total BTU is only meaningful in context — a well-insulated cooking chamber with tightly fitting grates will outperform a higher-BTU grill with poor heat retention. Factor in cooking area alongside BTU when comparing models.

Which subcategory pages should I check based on how I plan to use the grill?

The right starting point depends on your setup. If you are building or upgrading a full outdoor kitchen, Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen covers built-in and island-compatible options in depth. For a broader look at standalone and semi-portable natural gas units, Best Outdoor Grills Natural Gas is the most comprehensive overview. Buyers focused specifically on backyard barbecue performance should see Best Barbeque Grills Natural Gas or Best Barbecue Grills Natural Gas. If compatibility with an existing natural gas hookup is the primary concern, Best Grills For Natural Gas filters by that criterion directly.

Are stainless steel grills actually more durable outdoors, and which grade matters?

Stainless steel resists rust and surface corrosion far better than painted steel in outdoor environments, but the grade determines how well it holds up over years of use. Grade 304 stainless — used in the Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head — contains higher nickel and chromium content than the more common 430 grade, making it more resistant to pitting in humid or coastal environments. For a grill that will stay permanently installed and exposed to weather year-round, 304 stainless is worth the premium. For covered or seasonal use, 430 stainless performs adequately at a lower cost.

The Monument Grills Larger 4+2 Burner Propane Gas Grills Stainless Steel Cabinet Style with Clearview® Lid, Knob Controls, Built-In Thermometer, Infrared Side Sear Burners & Side Burners, Blue is the most affordable option at $519 and packs infrared side sear burners alongside two additional side burners — a feature set that typically appears on grills costing considerably more. Its Clearview® lid and 5/5 rating from 71 buyers make it a compelling freestanding alternative. Browse additional options in the Best Outdoor Grills Natural Gas and Best Barbeque Grills Natural Gas roundups.

Common questions in this category

Is a natural gas grill actually worth it over propane?

For anyone who cooks outdoors regularly, a direct natural gas connection eliminates the hassle of monitoring and swapping propane tanks — fuel is always on and the cost per BTU is typically lower than propane over time. The trade-off is that the grill must sit within reach of a gas line, so placement is fixed rather than flexible. If your outdoor space is permanent and you grill more than a few times a month, the convenience and long-term savings usually justify the conversion or upfront purchase.

See the top-rated natural gas outdoor grills →

What should you look for when buying a built-in grill for an outdoor kitchen?

Built-in grills need to fit a specific cabinet cutout, hold up to weather without a cover-friendly design, and be made from marine-grade or 304 stainless steel so they don't rust flush against surrounding stonework or cabinetry. BTU output, cooking area, and whether the unit ships ready for natural gas (or requires a conversion kit) are the other critical specs to confirm before ordering. A grill head rated for outdoor kitchen installation is not interchangeable with a freestanding unit — the ventilation, trim, and mounting requirements differ substantially.

Explore the best gas grills for outdoor kitchens →

How many BTUs do you actually need for outdoor grilling?

BTU ratings are useful for comparison but misleading in isolation — a well-designed 40,000 BTU grill with good heat retention will outperform a poorly built 60,000 BTU model. A practical rule of thumb is 80–100 BTUs per square inch of primary cooking area, which means a 400 sq. in. grill needs roughly 32,000–40,000 BTUs to sear and recover temperature quickly. Side burners, infrared sear zones, and griddle surfaces each carry separate BTU specs and should be evaluated independently from the main burner output.

Browse high-BTU natural gas barbecue grills →

Frequently asked questions

What is the difference between a built-in grill head and a freestanding natural gas grill?

A built-in grill head drops into a cutout in an outdoor kitchen island or counter, leaving only the cooking surface exposed. Models like the Hygrill STD Series and the Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head are designed specifically for this installation. Freestanding grills sit on their own cart or legs and can be repositioned. If you are planning a permanent outdoor kitchen, a built-in head gives a cleaner look and is generally easier to plumb to a natural gas line. If flexibility matters more than aesthetics, a freestanding or semi-portable option is the better fit.

Do I need a dedicated natural gas line, or can I convert a propane grill?

Most heavy-duty grills in this category are either factory-configured for natural gas or sold as convertible units. The Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head, for example, is listed as natural gas convertible, and the Brand-Man 4-Burner Grill Island is rated for both LPG and NG. Conversion typically requires a separate orifice kit and, in most municipalities, a licensed plumber or gas fitter to tap into the supply line. Converting without proper hardware voids warranties and creates a safety hazard, so verify the kit is included or available before purchasing a propane-primary model.

How do I choose between a grill head only and a full grill island station?

A grill head is the right buy when you already have an island structure or are building a custom outdoor kitchen with specific cabinet and countertop materials. A full island station, like the Brand-Man 4-Burner Grill Island with storage cabinets and fold-out prep table or the Tytus Fresno 5-Burner Island Grill, bundles storage, prep space, and sometimes a side burner into one purchase. Island stations cost less than sourcing each component separately but limit your design choices. The trade-off is convenience and speed of setup versus full customization.

What BTU output should I look for in a heavy-duty outdoor gas grill?

For everyday grilling, roughly 10,000 BTU per burner is a reasonable baseline. The grills reviewed across this silo range from 34,000 BTU across two burners on the Stanbroil Built-In Griddle to 48,000 BTU on the Tytus Fresno main burners plus a 12,000 BTU side burner. Higher BTU matters most for searing and recovering temperature quickly after you open the lid. However, total BTU is only meaningful in context — a well-insulated cooking chamber with tightly fitting grates will outperform a higher-BTU grill with poor heat retention. Factor in cooking area alongside BTU when comparing models.

Which subcategory pages should I check based on how I plan to use the grill?

The right starting point depends on your setup. If you are building or upgrading a full outdoor kitchen, Best Gas Grills For Outdoor Kitchen covers built-in and island-compatible options in depth. For a broader look at standalone and semi-portable natural gas units, Best Outdoor Grills Natural Gas is the most comprehensive overview. Buyers focused specifically on backyard barbecue performance should see Best Barbeque Grills Natural Gas or Best Barbecue Grills Natural Gas. If compatibility with an existing natural gas hookup is the primary concern, Best Grills For Natural Gas filters by that criterion directly.

Are stainless steel grills actually more durable outdoors, and which grade matters?

Stainless steel resists rust and surface corrosion far better than painted steel in outdoor environments, but the grade determines how well it holds up over years of use. Grade 304 stainless — used in the Brand-Man 30-Inch Built-In Head — contains higher nickel and chromium content than the more common 430 grade, making it more resistant to pitting in humid or coastal environments. For a grill that will stay permanently installed and exposed to weather year-round, 304 stainless is worth the premium. For covered or seasonal use, 430 stainless performs adequately at a lower cost.

Are stainless steel grills actually more durable outdoors, and which grade matters?

Stainless steel resists rust and surface corrosion far better than painted steel in outdoor environments, but the grade determines how well it holds up over years of use. For a grill that will stay permanently installed and exposed to weather year-round, 304 stainless is worth the premium. title: Outdoor Kitchens Grills
